author: Gaëtan Gilbert
message_subject: Stop relying on `replace by` automatic `assumption`-based solving (#1657)
message_body:
Before coq/coq#17964 `replace foo with bar by tac` actually means
`replace foo with bar by first [assumption | symmetry; assumption | tac]`.

message_subject: add comment about encoding of c; rename limbs, l into n

author: David Benjamin
message_subject: Add a constant-time binary extended GCD algorithm.
message_body:
Thankfully, most modular inverses involve a prime or at least odd
modulus, but RSA key generation requires inverting with an even modulus
(the totient). Implementing that in constant-time is tricky.

This file adapts the binary extended GCD algorithm in the Handbook of
Applied Cryptography. This is to verify the C implementation in
https://boringssl-review.googlesource.com/c/boringssl/+/26370.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Make the dag available in ASM equiv rewrite passes (#1505)
message_body:
We don't currently use it, but this will allow us to augment `bound_expr` to make use of bounds in the dag and write passes that use this augmented bounds information. In theory, this may also allow us to do lazy revealing of expressions rather than needing to reveal them deeply up-front. This is split off so that it is possible to add more rewrite passes without conflicting too much with changes to how bounds are handled.

author: Jason Gross
message_body:
This allows us to check multiple assembly files without having to
resynthesize the PHOAS input, as suggested in
https://github.com/mit-plv/fiat-crypto/issues/1170#issuecomment-1076886198.

What remains is to update the Makefile generation to take advantage of
this.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Add more operations to parsing in the equivalence checker (#1197)
message_body:
We now support parsing `call`, `cmovb`, `cmp`, `db`, `dd`, `dq`, `dw`,
`je`, `jmp`, `mul`, `pop`, `push`, `rcr`, and `shrx`, and prefixes like
`rep ret`, `repz ret`, `repnz ret`.  We also support labels, `align`,
and `default rel`.  We should probably try to properly support a larger
fraction of https://www.nasm.us/xdoc/2.15.05/html/nasmdoc3.html at some
point.

We now parse instructions (opcodes) in a case-insensitive manner, though
many other places are still case-sensitive.

The stack is now computed based on push and pop instructions as well as
the adjustments to rsp, and supports aggregating multiple constant-sized
adjustments to rsp.

We don't yet support the `0F3h` syntax alternative for the hex `0xF3`,
mostly because I couldn't be bothered to write a parser for it right
now.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Finish almost all of the proof of combine_consts

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Parameterize over dereference_scalar in more places
message_body:
This will allow us to reuse the same code to load inputs and outputs,
when we want to enforce the spec of reading back and removing all of the
values and then saying that the resulting symbolic memory state is
empty.

Some of the proofs are not quite as complete as they were before, but I
hope to replace them soon with a more principled approach.

message_subject: Split off the non-bedrock-dependent part of equiv proofs
message_body:
This will hopefully make development a bit faster; these files are so
long.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: More progress on assembly equivalence proof
message_body:
The only thing that remains to be proven is that the runtime output
values that we get are the ones we expect to get.  I need to figure out
where to propogate this information from, but this proof is almost done!

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Finish proving/using that SymexLines scribles only over known memory
message_body:
What remains is doing the reverse of the final symex goal to deal with
the different representations of memory specs, splitting apart input and
output values via `++` (not 100% sure how to do this yet), and showing
that the outputs are mapped to the right values (also need to figure out
how to do this).

author: Jason Gross
message_body:
Now `show` and `show_lines` no longer take a `with_parens` argument, and
instead that functionality is generalized into `ShowLevel` / `show_lvl`.
This is the first step towards having more flexibility in parenthesizing
printed expressions, in a more principled way.  It's sort-of a
test-drive of a more uniform way of level-based printing.

message_body:
This should be enough rope to check equivalence of PHOASTs with assembly
expressions.  It's plausible we want more control over the calling
conventions from the command line, for example, specifying:
- what to do when we run out of registers / how arguments are stored on
  the stack
- support for a number of common calling conventions

We may also want to emit better headers for the assembly files, rather
than replicating the header of the input file.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Add Jade's version of redistributing balance
message_body: From #857

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Generalized sub/opp balance for unsaturated solinas
message_body:
Alternative to #857.

Rather than redistributing an existing encoding, this version simply
encodes a number using `partition` that, when added to the relevant
bounds on a limb-by-limb basis without carrying, gives something which
evaluates to the desired result.

The limb-by-limb addition ensures the bounds we want, and the encoding
scheme ensures the correct value.  Furthermore, we don't need to know
ahead-of-time how many copies of the prime we need to add. This is
pleasingly simple to me.

Additionally, as in #857, the tweak to make `to_bytes` work for p224
(and other primes with low limbs) and unsaturated solinas.  The tweak
here is a bit uglier than in #857, because balancing here doesn't
guarantee the same value under `eval`, only something congruent to that
value.  So we need to ensure that we're encoding at least one copy of
the prime.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Generate value_barrier wrappers for cmovznz code
message_body:
This is significant progress towards #748

Note that rather than checking for whether or not `OPENSSL_NO_ASM` is
defined, we instead check for the definition of a curve-specific
`*_NO_ASM` variable, where the prefix is the uppercased prefix of the
curve description.  This lets us not be tied to OpenSSL.  (I presume
that BoringSSL can do something like
```c
#ifdef OPENSSL_NO_ASM
#define FIAT_25519_NO_ASM
#define FIAT_P256_NO_ASM
[...]
#endif
```
)

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Add support for comments and stripping annotations
message_body:
After `Import Crypto.Language.PreExtra`, it's now possible to do `dlet _
:= ident.comment stuff in ...` and have `stuff` appear in the
synthesized code as a comment.  There's room for improvement with
regards to quoting and handling multi-line comments, but it works for
debugging purposes.

Note that bounds analysis does not add casts underneath comments, though
I believe let-lifting still happens, and the comments are still
definitely subject to rewriting.

If you want comments whose variable dependencies are eliminated during
the very last dead-code-elimination phase of the stringification
pipeline, use `ident.comment_no_keep`.  Both `ident.comment` and
`ident.comment_no_keep` have type `forall {A}, A -> unit`.

There are now also passes `StripAnnotations` and `StripAllAnnotations`;
the former takes an expression and input bounds, and strips cast
annotations which are guaranteed to be no-ops given the bounds; it's
proven to preserve `Wf` and `Interp`-relatedness.  The
`StripAllAnnotations` pass strips *all* cast annotations, even the ones
that might be doing necessary truncation or modulo computations, and
does not require input bounds.  It's proven to preserve `Wf` but has no
`Interp` proofs.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Some minor improvements to CLI.v & base conversion

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Allow eliminating dead variables in stringification
message_body: This is needed to get valid Go code

author: jadep
message_subject: make Cmd.v more manageable by moving things to other files

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Add a binary to produce convert_bases
message_body:
```
USAGE: ./src/ExtractionOCaml/base_conversion [--lang=LANGUAGE] [--static] [--no-primitives] curve_description src_n src_limbwidth dst_limbwidth machine_wordsize max inbounds_multiplier [function_to_synthesize*]

  LANGUAGE                The output language code should be emitted in.  Defaults to C if no language is given.  Case-sensitive.
                            Valid options are: C, Rust
  --static                Declare the functions as static, i.e., local to the file.
  --no-wide-int           Don't use integers wider than the bitwidth.
  --no-primitives         Suppress the generation of the bodies of primitive operations such as addcarryx, subborrowx, cmovznz, mulx, etc.
  curve_description       A string which will be prefixed to every function name generated
  src_n                   The number of limbs in the input
  src_limbwidth           The limbwidth of the input field element
  dst_limbwidth           The limbwidth of the field element to be returned
  machine_wordsize        The machine bitwidth (e.g., 32 or 64)
  max                     The upperbound (strict / exclusive) on the input field element
  inbounds_multiplier     The (improper) fraction by which the bounds of each limb are scaled
  function_to_synthesize  A space-separated list of functions that should be synthesized.  If no functions are given, all functions are synthesized.
                            Valid options are convert_bases
```

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Add an alternate implementation of cmovznz
message_body: To be used by Go

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Refactor the stringification and IR code a bit
message_body:
We now delegate the descriptions of when and where to cast to the
different languages, and let them have custom logic based on the
identifier.

This hopefully makes things a bit cleaner and more obvious, while also
making things more flexible.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Add a missing rule to mul-split rewriting
message_body: Needed for some of the primitives

author: jadep
message_subject: pass after code review: change names, add offset for address indexing, improve comments

message_subject: add special add-get-carry and add-with-get-carry clauses so that we can set variables for intermediate values; improve formatting of output in CompilerTest

message_subject: add Z_add_with_get_carry and replace magic number 2^64 with 2 ^ Semantics.width

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Remove the Gallina arguments to Z_cast and Z_cast2
message_body:
This is one step closer to having cast not be a special identifier.
What remains is removing cast_outside_of_range.

Unsurprisingly, but unfortunately, this required reworking a number of
the proofs that depended on the exact structure of casts.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Hard-code overflow behavior to be wrapping
message_body:
This is required for dropping the carry in the mul-split addition
rewrite rule.

We also insert a compile-time test into the C code for twos-complement
wrapping behavior.

Question/TODO: Should we insert a test or use, e.g., `Wrapping`, in the
Rust code?

Note that this weakens the guarantee we get from bounds analysis; now
our guarantee is that we perform correctly when doing twos-complement
wrapping on overflow, rather than that we perform correctly regardless
of overflow behavior (as long as it is well-defined).

Note also that we leave everything parameterized over the now-useless
`cast_outside_of_range` so that we don't have to change large amounts of
code just to support targeting bedrock2.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Add CPS'd versions of arithmetic operations
message_body:
This is primarily for perf testing purposes, so we can evaluate the time
spent in the rewriter vs cbv/vm/native/etc for the cps'd versions.

We evaluate vm_compute and native_compute by parameterizing everything
over a module of runtime definitions, which can be instantiated with
module-locked opaque constants, which are not unfolded by the vm.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Autocompute s and c in WBW Montgomery
message_body:
This allows us to support primes which are not a power of 2.

We also add p484 as an example.

To support this, I added a small parser combinator library which can
parse arithmetic expressions involving `()^*/+-` and decimal digits.

Closes #486
Closes #342

author: Jason Gross
message_body:
We now enforce that [ident.cast] commutes appropriately with [Z.opp] /
[ZRange.opp].  In particular, we perform normalization to ensure that
more of the range is positive than negative (and if we have a range like
`r[-x ~> x]`, then we ensure that the value being casted is
non-negative), before using the given cast-outside-of-range function.
Afterwards, we perform the relevant negation to set it back.

This makes a number of rewrite rules simpler and easier to prove,
because we won't have to ensure that the casts are the same pre- and
post- negation; it's guaranteed that we can mess around with negation as
much as we want and still have things work out fine.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Add placeholder rewrite rules for rewriting after bounds
message_body:
Currently, there aren't any interesting rewrite rules in the lists, but
this allows us to move rewrite rules that depend on cast behavior to
after bounds analysis.

Unfortunately, it takes a lot of time to build from Rewriter.v to the
standalone binaries, so it'll probably make sense to inline some stuff
for more rapid development.

Note that we also now have a tactic that does all of the evaluation for
rewrite rules (assuming the right [Arguments] commands have been
issued).

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Guarantee that casting always returns inrange
message_body:
We fuse the bounds relaxation pass with the abstract interpretation pass
by folding bounds relaxation into the annotation method.  This allows us
to not need any particular assumptions about what happens when you cast
outside the range.

We can therefore now guarantee that the output of casting is always
bounded by the given range (rather, by the normalized version of the
range), which will be required to take advantage of bounds information
in the rewriter (because otherwise we're not guaranteed that casting
something to within a range actually outputs something within that
range).

The names are terrible and the code organization isn't great, and I
don't like the long list of [Let] statements which are morally doing
very similar things, but, it works.

author: Andres Erbsen
message_body: * clean up src/Experiments/Loops.v, add While and For

author: Jason Gross
message_body:
* [demo] Add reification in src/Experiments/SimplyTypedArithmetic.v

It's rather verbose, unfortunately.  The reification also doesn't have
any of the nice debugging features of the version of reification in
Compilers, because that's even more boilerplate.  Not sure if I should
add that back in, at the moment.

Also, for some strange reason, places where `constr`s fail to typecheck
seem to induce backtracking where I don't think they should, and I'm not
sure what's going on...

* [demo] Add more namespacing

* Update llet notation, update is_known_const name

As per code review suggestions

* Namespace var_context, add some coqbug references

* Rename is_type_arg to is_template_parameter

As per https://github.com/mit-plv/fiat-crypto/pull/275#discussion_r153036059

* Simplify the logic around delayed arguments a bit

We no longer pass around dummy markers in the tuple of arguments.

* [demo] More informative reification error messages

This time without exponential slowdown in failure cases and without
needing to manually think up all of the possible errors and write them
out.

Possible thanks to Hugo's comment at
https://github.com/coq/coq/issues/6252#issuecomment-347041995

* [demo] respond to code review, add comments

* Update documentation with suggestions from Andres

author: Jason Gross
message_body:
It now lives in Arithmetic.Core.B.Positional, where it belongs, rather
than in Specific/.../HelperTactics.

Andres notes that we probably don't need this at all, and could instead
make chained_carries reduce after every index (and the spurious
reductions should be no-ops).  I didn't want to bother verifying this,
at the moment, so I left it as-is.

author: Jason Gross
message_body:
This brings in most of the changes that I made when figuring out how to
integrate montgomery into the framework.

The code is a bit slower because the we drop `Print Assumptions` at the
bottom of each synthesis problem, to record that things are closed under
the global context.  If we remove this, we get back the time that we
lost with this commit.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Factor out some of the preglue synthesis code
message_body:
This makes it a bit more uniform, and hopefully more automatable and
packageable.  Unfortunately, there's still no spec for this part of the
pipeline, so the tactics simply aggregate common patterns.

Alas, this also makes things a bit slower; I suspect that [Defined] is
the place where things are slower.

author: Jason Gross
message_body:
This closes #171.

It's unfortunately a bit fragile, and takes a really long time (about
60s) to prove correct, because Coq is bad at deep dependent pattern
matching.

We enable a-normal form for the freeze test, because the rewriter only
works when the arguments to adc are var or const.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: First pass at bounded version of GF25519
message_body:
Boundedness is axiomatized as
```coq
Axiom ExprBinOp : Type.
Axiom ExprUnOp : Type.
Axiom interp_bexpr : ExprBinOp -> Specific.GF25519.fe25519 ->
Specific.GF25519.fe25519 -> Specific.GF25519.fe25519.
Axiom interp_uexpr : ExprUnOp -> Specific.GF25519.fe25519 ->
Specific.GF25519.fe25519.
Axiom radd : ExprBinOp.
Axiom rsub : ExprBinOp.
Axiom rmul : ExprBinOp.
Axiom ropp : ExprUnOp.
Axiom rinv : ExprUnOp.
Axiom radd_correct : forall x y, interp_bexpr radd x y = carry_add x y.
Axiom rsub_correct : forall x y, interp_bexpr rsub x y = carry_sub x y.
Axiom rmul_correct : forall x y, interp_bexpr rmul x y = mul x y.
Axiom ropp_correct : forall x, interp_uexpr ropp x = carry_opp x.
Axiom rinv_correct : forall x, interp_uexpr rinv x = inv x.
Axiom check_bbounds : ExprBinOp -> bool.
Axiom check_ubounds : ExprUnOp -> bool.
Axiom radd_bounded : check_bbounds radd = true.
Axiom rsub_bounded : check_bbounds rsub = true.
Axiom rmul_bounded : check_bbounds rmul = true.
Axiom ropp_bounded : check_ubounds ropp = true.
Axiom rinv_bounded : check_ubounds rinv = true.
Axiom bbounds_correct
  : forall rexpr, check_bbounds rexpr = true
                  -> forall x y, is_bounded x = true
                                 -> is_bounded y = true
                                 -> is_bounded (interp_bexpr rexpr x y)
= true.
Axiom ubounds_correct
  : forall rexpr, check_ubounds rexpr = true
                  -> forall x, is_bounded x = true
                               -> is_bounded (interp_uexpr rexpr x) =
true.
```

Questions:
- Some of this is very general and probably doesn't belong in this file
  (e.g., [correct_le_le]).  Should it stay, or move elsewhere?
- There's a lot of type munging that could be generalized to arbitrary
  heterogenous lists; should it be generalized?
- How do we do [div] on bounded things?  Currently, I have
```coq
Axiom div : forall (f g : fe25519), fe25519.
Axiom div_correct : forall a b : fe25519,
    ModularBaseSystem.eq (proj1_fe25519 (div a b))
                         (ModularBaseSystem.div (proj1_fe25519 a)
(proj1_fe25519 b)).
```

author: jadep
message_subject: Moved conversion logic out of Pow2BaseProofs into its own file

author: jadep
message_subject: Wrote proofs necessary to fill in all point-encoding related context variables in EdDSARepChange.v

author: Andres Erbsen
message_subject: add Montgomery x-coordinate Diffie-Hellman and Curve25519

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Make use of named syntax, do reg assign for fancy

author: Andres Erbsen
message_body: cleanup

author: jadep
message_subject: Reorganization, moving of lemmas to correct files, and 8.4 compatibility

author: jadep
message_subject: Fix the 8.4 build by changing a couple standard library names

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Add reserved notation for Let, change #
message_body:
We reserve [a # b] in Notations.v, and make it's level compatible with
the notation declared in the std lib for Q.

author: jadep
message_subject: Move side lemmas to appropriate files

author: jadep
message_subject: [freeze] proofs : Mostly-complete proofs of bounds after 3 carry loops

author: Robert Sloan
message_subject: Most of a more efficient WordRangeOpt

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Add Barrett and Montgomery for the 256-bit machine

author: jadep
message_subject: Pushed [freeze] through to GF25519 in preparation for defining [sqrt], cleaning up freeze-related organization and definitions along the way

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Add comments to WfReflective, handle Expr
message_body:
In addition to much better documentation in WfReflective, we now also have a
single tactic that handles goals of [Wf], on [Expr].

author: Jason Gross
message_body:
We also have linearization of function application / lets,
constant-inlining, and reification.

This closes #58.

author: jadep
message_subject: Added square roots to GF1305, started reworking freeze_opt in preparation for instantiating it in GF25519 (needed for equality comparison in sqrt_5mod8)

author: jadep
message_subject: square roots modulo p for [p mod 4 = 3]; we now have modular sqrt for all primes except 2 and primes that are 1 mod 8.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Add reduce via partial to Montgomery ZBounded

author: jadep
message_subject: Replaced placeholdeer [opp] operation in ModularBaseSystem with a real implementation, and pushed that up through Specific.

author: Jason Gross
message_body:
Now the rewrite strategy no longer relies on projections of anything
other than [decode], and the conversion to ZLike is simpler.

Modulo some annoyingly delicate arithmetic around things like [2^n * 2^n
= 2^(2*n)] and whether to factor [(decode (fst x) + decode (snd x) >> n)
>> b] as [decode x >> n] or as [shrd (fst x) (snd x) n], the proofs
bascially go by pulling/pushing decodes.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Initial work on an architecture interface for ℤ/nℤ
message_body:
This provides a cleaner interface for the bottom level implementation,
as well as an implementation of multiplying 128x128 -> 256.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Define Montgomery reduction / multiplication on Z (#42)
message_body:
This is partly done for my own benefit, to internalize how Montgomery
multiplication works, and partly done as a template for word-based
Montgomery multiplication when the carrying does not take advantage of
the fact that we are using a pseudomersenne prime.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Add a generalized version of Barrett Reduction (#44)
message_body:
In this version, we split up the integer division so that we are less
likely to overflow in intermediate computations.

This is still not the version in HAC 14.42; that version also does early
reduction modulo b^(k+1).

This is work towards #43

Terrible, horrible, no good, very bad bugs in Coq.
https://coq.inria.fr/bugs/show_bug.cgi?id=4966

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Make the library 20% faster: [auto with *] is evil
message_body:
I do hereby revoke the privilege of [intuition] to grab random hints
from random databases.  This privilege is reserved for
[debug_intuition], which comes with a warning about not being used in
production code.  This tactic is useful in conjunction with `Print Hint
*`, to discover what hint databases the hints were grabbed from.

(Suggestions for renaming [debug_intuition] welcome.)

Any file using [intuition] must [Require Export
Crypto.Util.FixCoqMistakes.].  It's possible we could lift this
restriction by compiling [FixCoqMistakes] separately, and passing along
`-require FixCoqMistakes` to Coq.  Should we do this?
